subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Appeal Routes — Court of Appeal vs Divisional Court
fact_pattern: A party loses a final judgment in the Superior Court of Justice in a commercial action where the amount in dispute is $350,000. The party wishes to appeal. Counsel must determine whether the appeal goes to the Divisional Court or the Court of Appeal.
call_of_question: Which court has jurisdiction to hear an appeal from a final order of the Superior Court of Justice where the amount in dispute exceeds $50,000?

options.A: The Divisional Court hears all appeals from the Superior Court of Justice regardless of the amount.
options.B: The Court of Appeal for Ontario has jurisdiction under s.6 of the Courts of Justice Act to hear appeals from final orders of the Superior Court where the amount in dispute exceeds $50,000.
options.C: The Supreme Court of Canada has direct jurisdiction to hear appeals from the Superior Court of Justice in commercial matters over $50,000.
options.D: The Court of Appeal and the Divisional Court share concurrent jurisdiction over all appeals from the Superior Court of Justice.

explanation: Under s.6(1)(b) of the Courts of Justice Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. C.43, an appeal lies to the Court of Appeal from a final order of the Superior Court of Justice in a proceeding where the amount in dispute is more than $50,000. The Divisional Court's appellate jurisdiction under s.19 of the Courts of Justice Act is limited to appeals from final orders of the Superior Court where the amount in dispute does not exceed $50,000, and to interlocutory orders with leave. With $350,000 in dispute, the Court of Appeal is the correct appellate forum.
why_A_wrong: The Divisional Court does not hear all appeals from the Superior Court. Its jurisdiction under s.19 of the Courts of Justice Act is limited to final orders under $50,000 (and interlocutory orders with leave). The Court of Appeal has jurisdiction for final orders above $50,000.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Section 6 of the Courts of Justice Act gives the Court of Appeal jurisdiction over appeals from Superior Court final orders where the amount exceeds $50,000.
why_C_wrong: The Supreme Court of Canada does not have direct jurisdiction to hear appeals from the Superior Court of Justice. It hears appeals from provincial courts of appeal only, by leave.
why_D_wrong: The Court of Appeal and the Divisional Court do not share concurrent jurisdiction over all Superior Court appeals. Their respective jurisdictions are defined by the amount in dispute under ss.6 and 19 of the Courts of Justice Act.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Appeal Routes — Court of Appeal vs Divisional Court
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Courts of Justice Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. C.43, s.6(1)(b); s.19; Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.61

tested_concepts: Court of Appeal jurisdiction — s.6 CJA over $50,000
Divisional Court jurisdiction — s.19 CJA under $50,000
correct appellate forum for final Superior Court orders

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Leave to Appeal — When Required and the Test
fact_pattern: A party wishes to appeal an interlocutory order of the Superior Court of Justice. Counsel advises that leave to appeal is required. The party asks what test the appellate court applies when determining whether to grant leave to appeal from an interlocutory order.
call_of_question: Under s.19(1)(b) of the Courts of Justice Act, what is the test for obtaining leave to appeal an interlocutory order of the Superior Court of Justice to the Divisional Court?

options.A: Leave is granted as a matter of right for all interlocutory orders; the only requirement is that the appeal be filed within 30 days.
options.B: Leave is granted where the proposed appeal raises an arguable point of law of general importance that ought to be decided by an appellate court, or there are conflicting decisions that need to be resolved.
options.C: Leave is granted only where the interlocutory order causes irreparable harm to the moving party pending trial.
options.D: Leave to appeal is not available for interlocutory orders; only final orders of the Superior Court may be appealed.

explanation: Under s.19(1)(b) of the Courts of Justice Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. C.43, an appeal lies to the Divisional Court from an interlocutory order of the Superior Court of Justice, with leave. Courts have consistently applied a test for leave that asks whether the proposed appeal raises an arguable point of law of general importance to the administration of justice, or whether there are conflicting decisions among trial courts that ought to be resolved, or whether the order is clearly wrong. The test is designed to filter out appeals that do not raise issues of broader importance while permitting genuine legal questions to proceed.
why_A_wrong: Leave to appeal interlocutory orders is not granted as of right. Section 19(1)(b) of the Courts of Justice Act requires the moving party to obtain leave, applying a discretionary test.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The test for leave to appeal an interlocutory order asks whether the appeal raises an arguable point of law of general importance or presents conflicting decisions that need resolution.
why_C_wrong: Irreparable harm is a factor in interlocutory injunction motions, not the test for leave to appeal an interlocutory order. The leave test focuses on legal importance and arguability.
why_D_wrong: Interlocutory orders are appealable to the Divisional Court with leave under s.19(1)(b) of the Courts of Justice Act. Leave to appeal is available for interlocutory orders.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Leave to Appeal — When Required and the Test
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Courts of Justice Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. C.43, s.6(1)(b); s.19(1)(b)

tested_concepts: leave to appeal interlocutory orders — s.19(1)(b) CJA
test for leave — arguable point of general importance
filtering interlocutory appeals

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Standard of Review on Appeal — Questions of Law vs Fact
fact_pattern: On appeal, the appellant argues that the trial judge made two errors: (1) incorrectly applying the legal test for breach of fiduciary duty — a question of law; and (2) incorrectly finding that the defendant was aware of the plaintiff's reliance — a finding of fact. Counsel must advise on the appropriate standard of review for each issue.
call_of_question: Under Housen v. Nikolaisen, 2002 SCC 33, what standard of review applies to questions of law on appeal compared to questions of fact?

options.A: Both questions of law and questions of fact are reviewed on a standard of correctness on appeal.
options.B: Questions of law are reviewed on a standard of reasonableness, while questions of fact are reviewed on a standard of correctness.
options.C: Questions of law are reviewed on a standard of correctness; questions of fact are reviewed on a standard of palpable and overriding error, which is a highly deferential standard.
options.D: Both questions of law and questions of fact are reviewed on a standard of palpable and overriding error.

explanation: Under the standard of review framework established by the Supreme Court of Canada in Housen v. Nikolaisen, 2002 SCC 33, questions of law are reviewed on correctness — the appellate court owes no deference to the trial judge's legal conclusions and may substitute its own view. Questions of fact are reviewed on a standard of palpable and overriding error — the appellate court must identify a clear, plain, and determinative error that has affected the outcome before overturning a factual finding. This is a highly deferential standard that reflects the trial judge's privileged position as fact-finder.
why_A_wrong: Only questions of law are reviewed on correctness. Questions of fact attract the more deferential palpable and overriding error standard under Housen v. Nikolaisen.
why_B_wrong: The standards are reversed in this option. Questions of law are reviewed on correctness (not reasonableness); questions of fact are reviewed on palpable and overriding error (not correctness).
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under Housen v. Nikolaisen: questions of law are reviewed on correctness; questions of fact are reviewed on the deferential palpable and overriding error standard.
why_D_wrong: Questions of law are not reviewed on palpable and overriding error. Only factual findings attract this deferential standard. Legal questions are reviewed on correctness under Housen.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Standard of Review on Appeal — Questions of Law vs Fact
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Housen v. Nikolaisen, 2002 SCC 33; Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.61

tested_concepts: Housen v. Nikolaisen 2002 SCC 33 — standards of review
questions of law — correctness standard
questions of fact — palpable and overriding error

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Standard of Review — Mixed Questions of Fact and Law
fact_pattern: On appeal, the appellant challenges the trial judge's determination that the defendant's conduct constituted negligence. The trial judge applied the correct legal test for negligence (duty, breach, causation, damage) but the appellant argues the judge misapplied the test to the specific facts found. Counsel must identify the applicable standard of review.
call_of_question: Under Housen v. Nikolaisen, 2002 SCC 33, what standard of review applies to mixed questions of fact and law?

options.A: Mixed questions of fact and law are always reviewed on a correctness standard because they involve an element of legal reasoning.
options.B: Mixed questions of fact and law are reviewed on the palpable and overriding error standard as a default; however, if a legal error can be extracted and isolated from the factual findings, that extracted legal question is reviewed on correctness.
options.C: Mixed questions of fact and law are reviewed on the reasonableness standard, giving deference to the trial judge's balancing of legal and factual considerations.
options.D: Mixed questions of fact and law are reviewed on a balance of probabilities standard by the appellate court.

explanation: Under the framework in Housen v. Nikolaisen, 2002 SCC 33, mixed questions of fact and law are reviewed on palpable and overriding error as the default standard, because such questions are typically dominated by factual findings and trial courts are best positioned to make them. However, where an extricable legal question can be isolated from the factual findings — for example, where the trial judge has incorrectly defined the legal test or applied an incorrect legal principle — that extracted legal question is reviewed on correctness. The challenge in the fact pattern involves the application of a correct legal test to facts, which is a classic mixed question reviewed on palpable and overriding error absent an extricable legal error.
why_A_wrong: Mixed questions of fact and law are not always reviewed on correctness. The default is palpable and overriding error; correctness applies only where an extricable legal question can be isolated.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The default standard for mixed questions is palpable and overriding error, with correctness applying only to extricable legal questions isolated from the factual findings.
why_C_wrong: Reasonableness is the standard in administrative law review, not in civil appellate review. Housen v. Nikolaisen does not apply a reasonableness standard to mixed questions in civil appeals.
why_D_wrong: Balance of probabilities is a standard of proof at trial, not a standard of appellate review. Appellate courts do not re-weigh evidence on a balance of probabilities standard.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Standard of Review — Mixed Questions of Fact and Law

tested_concepts: mixed questions default — palpable and overriding error
extricable legal question reviewed on correctness
Housen v. Nikolaisen 2002 SCC 33

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Fresh Evidence on Appeal — Palmer Test
fact_pattern: After losing at trial, the appellant discovers a document that was not produced at trial and now seeks to introduce it on appeal as fresh evidence. The document was in the possession of a third party and was not obtained by the appellant before trial, although the appellant acknowledges that a request for it could have been made during discovery.
call_of_question: Under the Palmer test for the admission of fresh evidence on appeal, which criterion is the appellant most likely to fail to satisfy?

options.A: The credibility criterion — the fresh evidence must be credible and probably accurate.
options.B: The relevance criterion — the fresh evidence must be relevant to a decisive issue at trial.
options.C: The due diligence criterion — the fresh evidence could not have been obtained with reasonable diligence before trial; the appellant's failure to request the document during discovery means this criterion is not met.
options.D: The decisive criterion — the fresh evidence must be conclusive on a key issue and is therefore too speculative to meet the standard.

explanation: Under the Palmer test, derived from Palmer v. The Queen, [1980] 1 SCR 759 and applied in Ontario civil appeals, fresh evidence must satisfy four criteria to be admitted on appeal: (1) due diligence — the evidence could not have been obtained with reasonable diligence before trial; (2) relevance — the evidence must be relevant to a decisive issue; (3) credibility — the evidence must be apparently credible; and (4) decisiveness — the evidence must be such that if believed it could reasonably be expected to have affected the result. In the scenario, the appellant acknowledges that a request for the document could have been made during discovery. This failure to exercise due diligence is fatal to the first Palmer criterion and will likely result in refusal to admit the fresh evidence.
why_A_wrong: The credibility criterion requires the fresh evidence to be apparently credible. This is often the easiest Palmer criterion to satisfy for a document, as documents are generally credible on their face. The due diligence criterion is the one most likely to be failed here.
why_B_wrong: The relevance criterion requires the evidence to relate to a decisive issue. Whether the document is relevant depends on its content, but the fact pattern does not suggest it lacks relevance. The due diligence failure is the critical problem.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The Palmer test requires due diligence — the evidence must not have been obtainable with reasonable effort before trial. Acknowledging the document could have been requested during discovery defeats this criterion.
why_D_wrong: The decisive criterion requires that the fresh evidence, if believed, could reasonably be expected to have affected the result. This is a reasonable threshold, not a requirement that the evidence be conclusive or certain. The due diligence failure is the critical barrier.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Fresh Evidence on Appeal — Palmer Test
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Palmer v. The Queen, [1980] 1 SCR 759; Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.61

tested_concepts: Palmer test for fresh evidence on appeal
four criteria: due diligence, relevance, credibility, decisiveness
due diligence — failure to request in discovery is fatal

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Stay of Execution Pending Appeal — Test and Procedure
fact_pattern: Nakamura Electronics Inc. loses a commercial judgment for $800,000 at trial and intends to appeal to the Court of Appeal. Nakamura asks its counsel whether it must pay the judgment or whether it can stay execution pending appeal. Counsel must advise on the applicable test for obtaining a stay.
call_of_question: What is the test an appellant must satisfy to obtain a stay of execution of a money judgment pending appeal in Ontario?

options.A: The filing of a notice of appeal automatically stays execution of the judgment pending the appeal; no motion is required.
options.B: To obtain a stay of execution pending appeal, the moving party must satisfy the three-part test from RJR-MacDonald: (1) there is a serious question to be tried (or a non-frivolous appeal); (2) the moving party will suffer irreparable harm if the stay is not granted; and (3) the balance of convenience favours granting the stay.
options.C: A stay of execution pending appeal is available only for injunctions and equitable orders, not for money judgments.
options.D: To obtain a stay, the appellant need only demonstrate that the appeal is not frivolous; no other factors are considered.

explanation: Under r.63.02 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194) and the established test drawn from RJR-MacDonald Inc v Canada (AG), [1994] 1 SCR 311, a party seeking a stay of execution of a judgment pending appeal must satisfy three criteria: (1) a serious question to be tried — whether the appeal raises a non-frivolous or arguable issue; (2) irreparable harm — the moving party will suffer irreparable harm if the stay is not granted and must pay the judgment before the appeal is resolved; and (3) balance of convenience — which party will suffer greater harm from granting or refusing the stay. The filing of a notice of appeal alone does not automatically stay a money judgment; a motion for a stay is required. Courts weigh all three factors and have discretion to impose conditions, such as requiring security.
why_A_wrong: Filing a notice of appeal does not automatically stay execution of a money judgment in Ontario. A motion for a stay of execution is required, and the appellant must satisfy the three-part test.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. A stay of execution pending appeal requires satisfying the three-part RJR-MacDonald test: serious question, irreparable harm, and balance of convenience.
why_C_wrong: Stays of execution are available for money judgments as well as equitable orders. The RJR-MacDonald test applies broadly to stay motions pending appeal in civil proceedings.
why_D_wrong: A non-frivolous appeal alone is insufficient to obtain a stay. The moving party must also demonstrate irreparable harm and that the balance of convenience favours a stay. All three parts of the test must be addressed.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Stay of Execution Pending Appeal — Test and Procedure
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.63.02; RJR-MacDonald Inc v Canada (AG), [1994] 1 SCR 311

tested_concepts: stay of execution pending appeal — r.63.02
three-part RJR-MacDonald test
notice of appeal does not automatically stay judgment

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Divisional Court Appeal — Final Orders Under $50,000 and Statutory Appeals
fact_pattern: A claimant obtains a final judgment against a contractor for $38,000 in the Superior Court of Justice. The contractor wishes to appeal the judgment. Counsel must identify the correct court to which the appeal lies.
call_of_question: Under s.19 of the Courts of Justice Act, which court hears appeals from final orders of the Superior Court of Justice where the amount in dispute does not exceed $50,000?

options.A: The Court of Appeal for Ontario, as it has jurisdiction over all appeals from the Superior Court of Justice.
options.B: The Divisional Court, which under s.19(1)(a) of the Courts of Justice Act hears appeals from final orders of the Superior Court where the amount in dispute does not exceed $50,000.
options.C: The Small Claims Court, as a final judgment of $38,000 falls within its monetary jurisdiction.
options.D: The Ontario Court of Justice (formerly Provincial Court), which hears all monetary claims under $50,000.

explanation: Under s.19(1)(a) of the Courts of Justice Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. C.43, an appeal lies to the Divisional Court from a final order of the Superior Court of Justice made without a jury in a proceeding where the amount in dispute does not exceed $50,000. With a final judgment of $38,000, the amount in dispute falls below the $50,000 threshold and the appeal lies to the Divisional Court, not the Court of Appeal. The Court of Appeal's jurisdiction under s.6 is reserved for final orders where the amount in dispute exceeds $50,000.
why_A_wrong: The Court of Appeal does not have jurisdiction over all appeals from the Superior Court. Under ss.6 and 19 of the Courts of Justice Act, the appellate forum is determined by the amount in dispute: under $50,000 goes to the Divisional Court; over $50,000 goes to the Court of Appeal.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Section 19(1)(a) of the Courts of Justice Act gives the Divisional Court jurisdiction over appeals from final Superior Court orders where the amount does not exceed $50,000.
why_C_wrong: The Small Claims Court is a trial court, not an appellate court. Appeals from Small Claims Court judgments go to the Divisional Court. The Small Claims Court does not hear appeals from the Superior Court.
why_D_wrong: The Ontario Court of Justice does not hear appeals from the Superior Court of Justice. It is a separate trial-level court with its own jurisdiction. Appeals from Superior Court final orders go either to the Divisional Court or the Court of Appeal depending on the amount.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Divisional Court Appeal — Final Orders Under $50,000 and Statutory Appeals
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Courts of Justice Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. C.43, s.19(1)(a)

tested_concepts: s.19(1)(a) CJA — Divisional Court for final orders under $50,000
contrast with Court of Appeal jurisdiction under s.6
correct appellate forum based on amount in dispute

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Notice of Appeal — Time to Appeal and Extending Time
fact_pattern: A final judgment dismissing the plaintiff's negligence action was released on April 1, 2026. The plaintiff's counsel received the endorsement on April 1 but, believing the 30-day period ran from the date the formal written order was signed and entered — which occurred on April 16 — did not serve and file a notice of appeal until May 14, 2026. The respondent moves to quash the appeal as out of time. The appellant concedes the notice of appeal was served 43 days after the date of the judgment and brings a cross-motion under r.3.02 to extend the time to appeal, arguing the error was understandable and the appeal raises a genuine legal issue.
call_of_question: Under r.61.04(1) of the Rules of Civil Procedure, when does the 30-day time to appeal begin to run, and what factors govern a court's decision whether to extend time under r.3.02?

options.A: The 30-day period runs from the date the formal order is signed and entered by the registrar; service on May 14 — 28 days after the April 16 entry date — is within time and the quash motion should be dismissed.
options.B: The time to appeal is 15 days from the date of the order for a final judgment in a negligence action; the notice of appeal filed on May 14 is out of time regardless of when the formal order was entered.
options.C: The time to appeal is 30 days from the date of the order under r.61.04(1), running from the date the order is made (April 1), not from the date of formal entry; the notice of appeal filed on May 14 is therefore 13 days late. The court may extend time under r.3.02, weighing the length of delay, the reason for delay, prejudice to the respondent, and whether the appeal raises a genuine issue of merit — and an extension is not automatic even for an understandable calendaring error.
options.D: The time to appeal is 60 days from the date of the order for final judgments; service on May 14 — 43 days after April 1 — is within the 60-day window and the notice of appeal is timely.

explanation: Under r.61.04(1) of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), a notice of appeal shall be served and filed within 30 days after the date of the order or decision appealed from. The 30-day clock begins on the date the order is made — here April 1, the date of judgment — not on the date the formal written order is signed or entered by the registrar. This is a common Wrong Timeline Trap: counsel who tracks time from the entry date (April 16) rather than the order date (April 1) will miscalculate the deadline. The notice of appeal served on May 14 is 43 days after April 1 and is therefore 13 days out of time. The court may extend the time to appeal under r.3.02 on such terms as are just, weighing: (1) the length of the delay; (2) the reason for the delay and whether it was inadvertent; (3) whether the proposed appeal raises a genuine issue of merit; (4) prejudice to the respondent from the extension; and (5) the overall interests of justice. An administrative or calendaring error is a relevant — but not automatically sufficient — reason; courts balance all factors and extensions are not granted as of course.
why_A_wrong: The 30-day period runs from the date the order is made, not from the date the formal written order is signed and entered. Tracking time from the entry date (April 16) is the most common calendaring error in appeals practice and is incorrect. Calculating 28 days from April 16 does not cure the fact that 43 days have elapsed since the April 1 judgment. (Wrong Timeline Trap — running time from entry date rather than order date)
why_B_wrong: There is no 15-day time to appeal for negligence actions or for any category of final order in Ontario civil proceedings. The standard period for appeals to the Court of Appeal under r.61.04(1) is 30 days for all final orders, without exception based on the type of claim. The 15-day figure is a fabricated shorter deadline designed to trap candidates who conflate appeal timelines with other short procedural windows. (Wrong Timeline Trap — 15 days does not exist for civil appeals)
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The 30-day period under r.61.04(1) runs from the date the order is made (April 1), not from formal entry. The notice of appeal filed May 14 is 13 days late, and any extension is governed by the multi-factor r.3.02 discretionary test — it is not automatic.
why_D_wrong: The time to appeal a final order to the Court of Appeal is 30 days, not 60 days. Sixty days is not the standard appeal period for civil final orders in Ontario. A candidate who conflates the 60-day period applicable in some other procedural contexts (such as certain statutory appeal timelines) with the r.61.04(1) period will be led to conclude the appeal is timely when it is not. (Wrong Timeline Trap — 60 days is not the r.61.04(1) period for civil appeals)

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Notice of Appeal — Time to Appeal and Extending Time
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.61.04(1); r.3.02

tested_concepts: 30-day time to appeal runs from date of order — not date of formal entry (r.61.04(1))
entry-date calendaring error — Wrong Timeline Trap
extension of time — r.3.02 multi-factor discretionary test
factors for extension: delay length, reason, prejudice, merit — not automatic
15-day and 60-day periods do not apply to civil final order appeals

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Scope of Appellate Powers — Affirm, Reverse, Vary, and New Trial
fact_pattern: On appeal from a trial judgment awarding $300,000 to the plaintiff, the Court of Appeal finds that the trial judge applied the correct legal test but made an error of fact that is palpable and overriding. The Court of Appeal believes that on the correct facts, the plaintiff should have been awarded only $180,000. Counsel for the respondent argues that the Court of Appeal must order a new trial and cannot simply substitute a different damages award.
call_of_question: Under s.134 of the Courts of Justice Act, what powers does the Court of Appeal have on appeal from a judgment, and may it substitute its own damages assessment without ordering a new trial?

options.A: The Court of Appeal may only affirm or reverse a trial judgment; it may not vary the judgment or substitute its own assessment of damages.
options.B: Under s.134 of the Courts of Justice Act, the Court of Appeal may affirm, reverse, or vary the order appealed from; it may substitute its own findings of fact and award different damages without ordering a new trial where the record is sufficient to enable it to do so.
options.C: The Court of Appeal may only order a new trial if it finds any error by the trial judge; it may not vary the quantum of a damages award.
options.D: The Court of Appeal must order a new trial whenever it finds a palpable and overriding error of fact; it has no power to substitute its own damages assessment.

explanation: Under s.134(1) of the Courts of Justice Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. C.43, the Court of Appeal may, on appeal from a final order: (a) affirm, vary, or set aside the order appealed from; (b) substitute its own decision for that of the court appealed from; (c) direct a new trial; or (d) make any other order that is just. Where the record is complete and sufficient to enable the Court of Appeal to assess damages without hearing fresh evidence, the court may substitute its own damages award rather than ordering a new trial. A new trial is ordered where a re-assessment of damages would require credibility determinations or other findings best left to a trial court. The fact that the record supports a different damages assessment of $180,000 may permit the Court of Appeal to vary the award directly.
why_A_wrong: The Court of Appeal has broad powers under s.134 CJA, including the power to vary a judgment and substitute its own findings. It is not limited to affirm or reverse.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Section 134 CJA gives the Court of Appeal power to affirm, vary, reverse, or substitute its own decision; it may assess damages directly where the record supports it.
why_C_wrong: The Court of Appeal is not required to order a new trial whenever it finds an error. It may vary the quantum of damages or substitute its own assessment where the record is sufficient.
why_D_wrong: A new trial is not mandatory on finding a palpable and overriding factual error. The Court of Appeal may substitute its own assessment of damages where the trial record is adequate for that purpose.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Scope of Appellate Powers — Affirm, Reverse, Vary, and New Trial
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Courts of Justice Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. C.43, s.134(1)

tested_concepts: s.134 CJA — Court of Appeal powers on appeal
may vary judgment and substitute damages directly
new trial ordered only where record insufficient

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Extending Time to Appeal — r.3.02 Factors
fact_pattern: A party failed to file a notice of appeal within 30 days of a final order. Forty-five days after the order, the party brings a motion under r.3.02 to extend the time to appeal. The party has a compelling legal argument on the merits of the appeal. The respondent argues that the delay of 15 days beyond the appeal period is too long to justify an extension.
call_of_question: On a motion to extend the time to appeal under r.3.02, is the length of the delay a decisive factor, or does the court consider it alongside other factors?

options.A: The length of the delay is the only relevant factor; any delay beyond 30 days from the order will result in refusal of the extension.
options.B: On a motion under r.3.02, the court considers the totality of circumstances including: the length of the delay, the reason for the delay, whether the proposed appeal raises a genuine issue of merit, and whether the respondent would suffer prejudice from an extension; no single factor is determinative and the court exercises a broad discretion.
options.C: The existence of a meritorious appeal argument is the only relevant factor; if the appeal has merit, an extension will always be granted regardless of the delay.
options.D: A motion under r.3.02 to extend time to appeal is only available where the delay is less than 14 days; longer delays are jurisdictionally barred.

explanation: Under r.3.02 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), the court may extend time on such terms as are just. On a motion to extend time to appeal, courts apply a multi-factor discretionary test. The relevant considerations include: (1) the length of the delay — a short delay is less problematic, but even a long delay is not automatically fatal; (2) the reason for the delay — an administrative error or inadvertence is treated more sympathetically than deliberate delay; (3) the merits of the proposed appeal — a frivolous appeal may not justify an extension while a genuinely arguable case supports one; (4) prejudice to the respondent from the extension — if the respondent has relied on the judgment in some material way; and (5) the overall interests of justice. No single factor is determinative; the court exercises a broad discretion to achieve a just result.
why_A_wrong: The length of the delay is one factor among many. Courts routinely grant extensions for delays of months where the other factors support it; a 15-day delay is unlikely to be decisive on its own.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Rule 3.02 motions involve a multi-factor discretionary test — delay length, reason, merits, and prejudice — with no single factor being determinative.
why_C_wrong: Merit is an important factor but it is not the only relevant consideration. Even a meritorious appeal may be denied an extension where the delay is very long and there is significant prejudice to the respondent.
why_D_wrong: There is no jurisdictional bar on r.3.02 motions for delays beyond 14 days. The rule provides a general discretionary power to extend time that applies to delays of any length, subject to the factors being weighed.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Extending Time to Appeal — r.3.02 Factors
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.3.02; r.61.04(1)

tested_concepts: r.3.02 — discretionary multi-factor test for extending time
factors: delay length, reason, merits, prejudice
no single factor is determinative

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Appellate Review of Discretionary Decisions — Deference
fact_pattern: A trial judge exercises discretion in granting an interlocutory injunction. On appeal, the appellant argues that the trial judge weighed the balance of convenience differently than the appellant would have preferred. The respondent argues that the Court of Appeal should defer to the trial judge's exercise of discretion.
call_of_question: What is the standard of appellate review for a trial judge's exercise of discretion, such as the decision to grant or refuse an interlocutory injunction?

options.A: Appellate courts review discretionary decisions on a correctness standard; the Court of Appeal may freely substitute its own view of how the discretion should have been exercised.
options.B: Discretionary decisions are reviewed deferentially; an appellate court will only intervene where the trial judge misdirected himself or herself in law, proceeded on a wrong principle, or reached a result so clearly wrong that it can be characterized as an injustice.
options.C: Discretionary decisions are never subject to appellate review; an exercise of discretion is final and binding regardless of the reasoning.
options.D: Discretionary decisions are reviewed on a balance of probabilities; the appellate court determines whether the trial judge more probably than not exercised the discretion correctly.

explanation: Under Housen v. Nikolaisen, 2002 SCC 33, In Ontario and across Canada, appellate courts apply a highly deferential standard when reviewing a trial judge's exercise of discretion. An appellate court will not interfere with a discretionary decision simply because it would have weighed the factors differently. Intervention is justified only where: (1) the trial judge misdirected himself or herself in law (applied the wrong legal principles); (2) the trial judge clearly proceeded on a wrong principle; or (3) the decision is so clearly wrong as to amount to an injustice. This high threshold reflects that discretionary decisions depend on the judge's weighing of contextual factors, which the trial judge is best positioned to assess. A mere difference of opinion about how the balance of convenience should have been assessed is not a sufficient ground for appellate intervention.
why_A_wrong: Discretionary decisions are not reviewed on a correctness standard. The correctness standard applies to pure questions of law. Discretionary decisions attract a highly deferential standard of review.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Discretionary decisions are reviewed deferentially; appellate intervention requires misdirection in law, wrong principle, or a result so clearly wrong as to be unjust.
why_C_wrong: Discretionary decisions are subject to appellate review but only on a limited and deferential basis. They are not entirely immune from review; misdirection in law and clear injustice justify intervention.
why_D_wrong: Balance of probabilities is a standard of proof, not a standard of appellate review. Appellate review of discretionary decisions applies a deferential standard based on the grounds for intervention described above.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Appellate Review of Discretionary Decisions — Deference
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Housen v. Nikolaisen, 2002 SCC 33; Courts of Justice Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. C.43, s.134

tested_concepts: appellate review of discretion — highly deferential
intervention: misdirection in law, wrong principle, or injustice
contrast with correctness standard for pure legal questions

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Costs on Appeal — General Rule and Non-Party Costs
fact_pattern: The respondent successfully defends an appeal to the Court of Appeal. The respondent seeks costs of the appeal. The Court of Appeal also considers whether to award costs against a non-party litigation funder who funded the appellant's unsuccessful appeal.
call_of_question: Under the general costs principle on appeal, who is ordinarily entitled to costs when an appeal is dismissed, and may an appellate court award costs against a non-party?

options.A: The successful respondent is entitled to costs as a matter of right; no non-party may ever be ordered to pay costs on appeal.
options.B: Under the costs-follow-the-event principle, the successful respondent is ordinarily entitled to costs of a dismissed appeal; an appellate court has jurisdiction to award costs against a non-party (such as a litigation funder) under its inherent jurisdiction and r.57.03, where the non-party has substantially controlled or funded the litigation and it is just to do so.
options.C: No costs are awarded on appeal; costs are only available at the trial level.
options.D: Costs on appeal are fixed at a flat rate of $5,000 for all appeals; no discretion exists.

explanation: Under Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.57.01, the costs-follow-the-event principle applies on appeal as it does at first instance: the successful party on appeal is ordinarily entitled to its costs of the appeal. Where an appeal is dismissed, the respondent who successfully defended the appeal is the successful party and is entitled to costs. Additionally, Ontario courts — including appellate courts — have jurisdiction to order costs against non-parties where the non-party has taken an active interest in the proceedings, substantially funded or controlled the litigation, and it is just to make such an order. Litigation funders who fund unsuccessful appeals have, in certain circumstances, been ordered to pay costs directly, particularly where the funder took active control of the litigation strategy.
why_A_wrong: The successful respondent is ordinarily entitled to costs as the costs-follow-the-event rule applies on appeal. However, the second part — that no non-party may ever be ordered to pay costs — is incorrect. Courts have jurisdiction to make non-party costs orders in appropriate circumstances.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Costs-follow-the-event applies on appeal; non-party costs orders (including against litigation funders) are within the court's jurisdiction where just to do so.
why_C_wrong: Costs are regularly awarded on appeal. The costs-follow-the-event principle applies at all levels of court, including appellate proceedings.
why_D_wrong: There is no fixed flat rate for costs on appeal. The court exercises discretion in fixing costs, applying the r.57.01 factors and considering the nature of the appeal, the complexity of issues, and other relevant considerations.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Costs on Appeal — General Rule and Non-Party Costs
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.57.01; r.57.03

tested_concepts: costs-follow-the-event applies on appeal
successful respondent entitled to costs of dismissed appeal
non-party costs orders available against litigation funders in appropriate cases

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Interlocutory Appeals — Which Court Hears Them
fact_pattern: A Superior Court judge makes an interlocutory order during the course of a commercial action refusing to grant the defendant leave to file additional affidavit evidence on a summary judgment motion. The defendant wishes to appeal the interlocutory order. Counsel must advise which court has jurisdiction.
call_of_question: Which court hears an appeal from an interlocutory order of the Superior Court of Justice in a civil proceeding, and is the appeal as of right or with leave?

options.A: The Court of Appeal hears all interlocutory appeals from the Superior Court as of right.
options.B: The Divisional Court hears appeals from interlocutory orders of the Superior Court of Justice; the appeal requires leave under s.19(1)(b) of the Courts of Justice Act.
options.C: The Ontario Court of Justice hears interlocutory appeals from the Superior Court.
options.D: Interlocutory orders of the Superior Court are not subject to appeal until after the final judgment is made in the action.

explanation: Under s.19(1)(b) of the Courts of Justice Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. C.43, an appeal lies to the Divisional Court from an interlocutory order of the Superior Court of Justice, with leave. An interlocutory order does not finally dispose of any issue in the action but is made in the course of the proceedings. The Court of Appeal does not have jurisdiction over interlocutory appeals from the Superior Court; that jurisdiction belongs to the Divisional Court. Leave is required — the appeal does not lie as of right — and the test for leave considers whether the proposed appeal raises an arguable point of law of general importance.
why_A_wrong: The Court of Appeal does not have jurisdiction over interlocutory appeals from the Superior Court. That jurisdiction belongs to the Divisional Court under s.19(1)(b) of the Courts of Justice Act.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Interlocutory appeals from the Superior Court go to the Divisional Court with leave under s.19(1)(b) CJA.
why_C_wrong: The Ontario Court of Justice has no appellate jurisdiction over Superior Court orders. It is a separate court at the trial level.
why_D_wrong: Interlocutory orders are subject to appeal during the proceedings, not only after final judgment. The mechanism is to seek leave to appeal to the Divisional Court under s.19(1)(b) CJA.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Interlocutory Appeals — Which Court Hears Them
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Courts of Justice Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. C.43, s.19(1)(b)

tested_concepts: interlocutory appeals — Divisional Court with leave s.19(1)(b)
Court of Appeal has no jurisdiction over interlocutory appeals from Superior Court
leave is required — not as of right

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Distinguishing Interlocutory from Final Orders — Practical Application
fact_pattern: A Superior Court judge dismisses a motion for summary judgment. The moving party argues the dismissal of the summary judgment motion is a final order because the judge made findings on the substantive issues in the case. The respondent argues it is an interlocutory order because the action continues.
call_of_question: Is the dismissal of a motion for summary judgment a final order or an interlocutory order for purposes of determining the correct appellate court?

options.A: The dismissal of a summary judgment motion is always a final order because it resolves the merits of the claim.
options.B: The dismissal of a motion for summary judgment is an interlocutory order because the action continues; the rights of the parties in the action have not been finally determined. The appeal lies to the Divisional Court with leave under s.19(1)(b) of the Courts of Justice Act.
options.C: The dismissal of a summary judgment motion is a final order if the judge made any findings of fact in the process of dismissing the motion.
options.D: The dismissal of a summary judgment motion may be appealed as of right to the Court of Appeal because it was argued at a full hearing.

explanation: Under Courts of Justice Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. C.43, s.19(1)(b), the distinction between an interlocutory and a final order depends on the effect of the order, not on the length or nature of the hearing. An order is final if it finally disposes of the rights of the parties in the action; it is interlocutory if it does not. When a summary judgment motion is dismissed, the action continues to trial — the rights of the parties have not been finally determined. The dismissal of summary judgment is therefore an interlocutory order, and the appeal lies to the Divisional Court with leave under s.19(1)(b) of the Courts of Justice Act. This is so even though the judge may have made factual findings in the course of dismissing the motion; those findings are not final because the issues proceed to trial. By contrast, if summary judgment is granted, that is a final order.
why_A_wrong: The dismissal of a summary judgment motion is not a final order. Because the action continues and the parties' rights have not been finally determined, the order is interlocutory.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Dismissal of summary judgment is interlocutory (the action continues); appeal lies to the Divisional Court with leave under s.19(1)(b) CJA.
why_C_wrong: Factual findings made in the course of dismissing a summary judgment motion do not make the order final. The character of the order depends on whether it finally disposes of the parties' rights, not on whether findings of fact were made.
why_D_wrong: The dismissal of a summary judgment motion does not give rise to an as-of-right appeal to the Court of Appeal. As an interlocutory order, the proper appellate route is the Divisional Court with leave.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Distinguishing Interlocutory from Final Orders — Practical Application
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Courts of Justice Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. C.43, s.19(1)(b)

tested_concepts: final order — finally disposes of parties' rights
interlocutory order — action continues
dismissal of summary judgment is interlocutory — Div Court with leave

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Grounds of Appeal — Error of Law, Fact, and Discretion
fact_pattern: An appellant's notice of appeal alleges three grounds: (1) the trial judge misapplied the legal test for the duty of care in negligence; (2) the trial judge's finding that the defendant drove through a red light was against the weight of the evidence; and (3) the trial judge's award of costs on a substantial indemnity basis was inappropriate. Counsel must advise which grounds are likely to succeed.
call_of_question: How does the standard of review affect the likelihood of success of each of the three grounds of appeal?

options.A: All three grounds are equally likely to succeed because the Court of Appeal reviews all aspects of a trial judgment on a correctness standard.
options.B: Ground 1 (error of law) is reviewed on correctness, giving the appellant the best chance of success; Ground 2 (finding of fact) requires showing a palpable and overriding error, which is a high threshold; Ground 3 (costs award) requires showing an error in principle or a clearly unjust result, since costs are discretionary and the Court of Appeal grants leave for costs appeals.
options.C: Grounds of fact are always the easiest to succeed on because appellate courts are in as good a position as the trial judge to assess documentary evidence.
options.D: Only legal errors can be raised on appeal; findings of fact and costs awards cannot be challenged at the appellate level.

explanation: Under Housen v. Nikolaisen, 2002 SCC 33, the standard of review determines the appellate court's approach to each ground: (1) Ground 1 — misapplication of the legal test for duty of care is a question of law reviewed on correctness. The appellant need only show the trial judge got the law wrong; no deference is owed. This is typically the strongest type of appellate ground. (2) Ground 2 — a finding that the defendant drove through a red light is a pure finding of fact. Under Housen v. Nikolaisen, 2002 SCC 33, the standard is palpable and overriding error, which is highly deferential. The appellate court will not interfere merely because it would weigh the evidence differently. (3) Ground 3 — a substantial indemnity costs award is a discretionary decision. An appeal of costs alone requires leave under s.133(b) of the Courts of Justice Act, and the standard is whether the trial judge erred in principle or reached a result so clearly unjust that appellate intervention is warranted.
why_A_wrong: The three grounds are not reviewed on the same standard. Error of law is reviewed on correctness; factual findings on palpable and overriding error; and discretionary costs awards on a principle-error or clear injustice standard. They have very different prospects of success.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The three grounds attract different standards: correctness for error of law, palpable and overriding error for facts, and error in principle/clear injustice for costs discretion.
why_C_wrong: Factual findings are the most difficult to disturb on appeal because of the highly deferential palpable and overriding error standard. Appellate courts do not re-assess documentary evidence de novo.
why_D_wrong: Both factual findings and costs awards may be challenged on appeal, but they are subject to deferential standards. Findings of fact may be overturned for palpable and overriding error; costs awards for error in principle.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Grounds of Appeal — Error of Law, Fact, and Discretion
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Housen v. Nikolaisen, 2002 SCC 33; Courts of Justice Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. C.43, s.133(b)

tested_concepts: different standards for different grounds of appeal
law — correctness; fact — palpable overriding; discretion — error in principle
costs appeal requires leave — s.133(b) CJA

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Leave to Appeal to Court of Appeal — Test for Granting Leave
fact_pattern: A party seeks leave to appeal a final order of the Superior Court to the Court of Appeal in a commercial matter. The amount in dispute is $45,000 — below the $50,000 threshold for an as-of-right appeal. The party argues that the proposed appeal raises an important question of commercial contract law that affects multiple parties in similar disputes.
call_of_question: When leave to appeal to the Court of Appeal is required, what is the test that must be satisfied?

options.A: Leave to appeal to the Court of Appeal is available only where the amount in dispute exceeds $250,000; $45,000 does not qualify.
options.B: Leave to appeal to the Court of Appeal is granted where the proposed appeal raises a question of law of general importance to the administration of justice or where there are conflicting decisions that should be resolved; the significance of the issue beyond the immediate parties is relevant.
options.C: Leave to appeal to the Court of Appeal is granted automatically if the trial judge made any legal error in the judgment.
options.D: Leave to appeal is not available to the Court of Appeal from final orders of the Superior Court; only the Divisional Court has jurisdiction over Superior Court final orders below $50,000.

explanation: Under Courts of Justice Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. C.43, s.6(1)(b), Where the amount in dispute falls below the $50,000 threshold that gives a right of appeal to the Court of Appeal under s.6(1)(b) of the Courts of Justice Act, a party may still seek leave to appeal to the Court of Appeal. Leave is granted where the proposed appeal raises a question of general importance to the administration of justice — that is, a question whose resolution would have significance beyond the immediate parties and would benefit the development of the law. The existence of conflicting trial or appellate decisions on the same question may also support a grant of leave. The importance of the commercial contract law question in the fact pattern — affecting similar disputes involving many parties — is precisely the kind of consideration that may justify a grant of leave.
why_A_wrong: There is no requirement that the amount in dispute exceed $250,000 for leave to appeal to the Court of Appeal. Leave may be sought whenever the amount falls below the as-of-right threshold, subject to the general importance test.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Leave to appeal to the Court of Appeal is granted where the proposed appeal raises a question of law of general importance to the administration of justice or resolves conflicting decisions.
why_C_wrong: Leave is not granted automatically for any legal error. The test focuses on the general importance of the question to the administration of justice, not merely whether any error was made.
why_D_wrong: Leave to appeal to the Court of Appeal is available from final orders of the Superior Court below $50,000. The Divisional Court has jurisdiction for such orders as of right (under s.19(1)(a)), but a party may also seek leave to go directly to the Court of Appeal on an important question of law.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Leave to Appeal to Court of Appeal — Test for Granting Leave
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Courts of Justice Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. C.43, s.6(1)(b); s.19

exam_trigger_words: leave to appeal Court of Appeal
tested_concepts: leave to appeal — question of general importance to administration of justice
conflicting decisions as basis for leave
leave available below $50,000 as-of-right threshold
